Expected an assignment or function call and instead saw an expression.
React Hook useEffect has a missing dependency: 'nextReview'. Either include it or remove the dependency array.

suggestions.desc: Update the dependencies array to be: [nextReview]

messages.message: Image elements must have an alt prop, either with meaningful text, or an empty string for decorative images.

import Image from 'next/image'; "use client"; import { useState, useEffect } from "react"; import Link from "next/link"; export default function Home() { const [showOptions, setShowOptions] = useState(false); const [currentReview, setCurrentReview] = useState(0); const toggleOptions = () => { setShowOptions(!showOptions); }; const reviews = [ { name: "Tanvir Ahmed", image: "https://i.pravatar.cc/150?u=tanvir", quote: '"Thank you very much for all materials. I have improved my listening score from 5.5 to 8, studying only your released materials!"', score: "8.0", }, { name: "Abdullah Al Mamun", image: "https://i.pravatar.cc/150?u=abdullah", quote: '"IELTS PRO BD is the best platform for preparation. The practice tests are exactly like the real exam. Highly recommended!"', score: "7.5", }, { name: "Faisal Karim", image: "https://i.pravatar.cc/150?u=faisal", quote: '"The speaking tools and expert feedback helped me gain confidence. My score jumped from 6.0 to 8.5 in just 2 months!"', score: "8.5", }, ]; const nextReview = () => { setCurrentReview((prev) => (prev + 1) % reviews.length); }; const prevReview = () => { setCurrentReview((prev) => (prev - 1 + reviews.length) % reviews.length); }; useEffect(() => { const interval = setInterval(nextReview, 5000); return () => clearInterval(interval); }, []); return (

{/* Hero Section */}
arrow_back Back to Listening Tips
Listening Tip schedule 4 min read

{title}

Sarah Jenkins

Senior IELTS Examiner

{/* Main Content Layout */}
{/* Main Article Content */}

Multiple choice questions (MCQs) in the IELTS Listening test are notorious for being tricky. They heavily rely on distractors—words or phrases designed to mislead you into choosing the wrong answer.

What is a Distractor?

A distractor is a piece of information that seems like it could be the correct answer based on what you hear, but is actually false or irrelevant when you listen to the full context. Speakers will often self-correct, hesitate, or mention all the options before settling on the real final answer.

"The key to mastering MCQs is not just listening for the words in the options, but understanding the meaning and the speaker's final intent."

3 Strategies to Overcome Distractors

1 Wait Before You Write

Do not immediately write down the first answer you hear that matches an option. The speaker might say: "We originally planned to meet at 3 PM, but then John had a conflict so we pushed it to 4 PM." If you just listened for a time, 3 PM is a distractor. Wait for the complete thought.

2 Listen for Contrast Words

Contrast words are massive red flags that a distractor was just used or is about to be corrected. Listen carefully for:

  • But
  • However
  • Actually
  • Instead of
  • On second thought

3 Highlight Keywords Before Listening

Before the recording begins, use your reading time to highlight the main noun and verb in the question stem. What exactly is the question asking for? If the question asks for the final decision, ignore any preliminary ideas discussed.

{/* Practical Example Box */}

headphones Practice Exercise

LISTEN TO THIS MOCK SNIPPET:

"I was thinking about taking the train because it's usually faster, but looking at the current timetable, it seems there are huge delays. So I've decided to just drive there, even though parking will be a nightmare."

Q: How will the speaker travel?

  1. A By train Distractor
  2. B By car Correct Answer
  3. C By walking

"Mastering Skimming & Scanning" : formattedTitle; return (
{/* Hero Section */}
arrow_back Back to Reading Tips
Reading Tip schedule 5 min read

{title}

Dr. Emily Chen

Head of Reading Strategy

{/* Main Content Layout */}
{/* Main Article Content */}

Time is your biggest enemy in the IELTS Reading module. To read 3 full passages in 60 minutes while answering 40 questions requires a completely different approach from casually reading a book.

The Difference Between Skimming & Scanning

While often grouped together, skimming and scanning are two completely distinct tools in your reading arsenal. Knowing when to use which is the secret to scoring a Band 8.0+.

"Skim for the main idea, scan for specific details, but read carefully to finalize your answer."

How to Skim Effectively

1 Read the First and Last Sentences

In academic texts, the first sentence is usually the topic sentence, and the final sentence provides a concluding or transitional thought. Reading just these two sentences of a paragraph will give you an 80% understanding of what the paragraph is about.

2 Ignore Unnecessary Words

Pass your eyes quickly over adjectives, adverbs, and complex descriptive clauses. Focus strictly on nouns and main verbs.

How to Scan like a Pro

Scanning is exactly what you do when you're looking for a name in a phone book. You don't read every entry; you let your eyes glide over the text until they lock onto a specific shape or trigger word.

  • Names of people (Capital letters)
  • Dates & Years (Numbers standing out)
  • Technical terms (Often in italics/quotes)
  • Locations (Capitalized nouns)
{/* Practical Example Box */}

search Scanning Exercise

SCAN THE TEXT BELOW FOR 'Dr. Harrison':

The early theories of cognitive mapping were heavily debated in academic circles. It wasn't until the groundbreaking research conducted by Dr. Harrison in 1984 that mainstream psychology began to adopt these spatial awareness mapping models as fundamental cognitive structures.

Insight: Notice how your eye automatically jumps to the capitalized 'D' and 'H'.

That is scanning in action. You did not need to understand 'cognitive mapping' to find the name.

{/* Article Tags */}
Skimming Scanning Time Management
{/* Sidebar */}
{/* Call to Action Widget */}

"Part 2: The 2-Minute Monologue" : formattedTitle; return (
{/* Hero Section */}
arrow_back Back to Speaking Tips
Speaking Tip schedule 7 min read

{title}

Jessica Reynolds

Former IELTS Speaking Examiner

{/* Main Content Layout */}
{/* Main Article Content */}

Part 2 of the Speaking test—often called the "long turn" or the "cue card" section—is where most candidates hit a mental wall. Staring at the examiner in silence after 45 seconds is everyone's worst nightmare.

The Truth About the Cue Card

The prompt gives you a topic (e.g., "Describe a memorable journey") and three bullet points to guide you. What most test-takers don't realize is that you do not have to strictly stick to the bullet points. They are suggestions, not strict requirements.

"The examiner is assessing your fluency, vocabulary, grammar, and pronunciation. They are not grading you on how many bullet points you checked off."

How to Ensure You Speak for 2 Minutes

1 The "Past, Present, Future" Strategy

If you finish answering the bullet points early, bridge your topic into different timeframes. Talk about how your experience with the topic was in the past, how it is now, and how you imagine it in the future.

2 Use the W-H Questions Framework

Expand any single bullet point by asking yourself: Who? What? Where? When? Why? How? Instead of just saying "I went with my brother," say "I went with my younger brother, who had actually never been on an airplane before, so I had to spend the first two hours just calming his nerves."

3 Tell a Story

Human brains are wired for narratives. Storytelling naturally strings sentences together logically and invites complex grammar (like past continuous and past perfect tenses). Don't just list facts; tell a mini-story about feelings, reactions, and outcomes.

{/* Practical Example Box */}

record_voice_over Answer Expansion Exercise

CUE: "Describe a restaurant you enjoyed visiting."

Rookie Answer (Stops after 10 seconds): "I went to an Italian restaurant downtown. The pizza was very good and the waiters were nice."

Band 8+ Answer (Detailed Storytelling): "There's this fantastic, authentic Italian trattoria tucked away in an alley downtown. I actually stumbled upon it completely by accident when trying to avoid a rainstorm last month. What immediately caught my attention wasn't just the incredible aroma of garlic and fresh basil, but the fact that the entire kitchen staff were shouting to each other in rapid Italian, which instantly gave it this wonderful, chaotic charm."

{/* Article Tags */}
Part 2 Long Turn Fluency Storytelling
{/* Sidebar */}
{/* Call to Action Widget */}

"Task 2: The Perfect Essay Structure" : formattedTitle; return (
{/* Hero Section */}
arrow_back Back to Writing Tips
Writing Tip schedule 10 min read

{title}

Prof. Marcus Thorne

Chief Writing Evaluator

{/* Main Content Layout */}
{/* Main Article Content */}

Most students lose points in Writing Task 2 not because they lack vocabulary, but because their ideas are jumbled and hard to follow. Coherence and Cohesion accounts for 25% of your score.

The Magic 4-Paragraph Framework

Instead of trying to reinvent the wheel, apply a strict 4-paragraph structure to every single essay you write. This limits the cognitive load required to organize your thoughts and lets you focus on answering the prompt.

"The examiner is not looking for a novel. They are looking for a clear argument, supported by structured logic."

Paragraph Breakdown

1 Introduction (2-3 Sentences)

Always begin by paraphrasing the prompt. Take the exact words of the prompt and swap out nouns and verbs using synonyms. The next sentence should be your Thesis Statement: clearly outline your opinion or what the essay will discuss.

2 Body Paragraph A (5-6 Sentences)

Present your first main idea. Follow this formula:

  • Topic Sentence: State the core idea clearly.
  • Explanation: Explain *why* this idea is true.
  • Example: Provide a specific, real-world example.
  • Result: Summarize the finding.

3 Body Paragraph B (5-6 Sentences)

Present your second main idea using the exact same four-step formula as above. Depending on the question type (e.g. Advantage/Disadvantage), this paragraph may present the opposing viewpoint.

4 Conclusion (1-2 Sentences)

Summarize your main points briefly and restate your thesis. Never introduce completely new ideas or arguments in your conclusion.

{/* Practical Example Box */}

edit_document Introduction Exercise

PROMPT: "Some people believe that university education should be free for everyone. Do you agree or disagree?"

Paraphrase: It is argued by some that tertiary education should be fully subsidized by the government without placing a financial burden on individuals.

Thesis: I completely agree with this notion, as it promotes social equality and ensures an educated workforce driving long-term economic growth.

{/* Article Tags */}
Task 2 Structuring Coherence & Cohesion
{/* Sidebar */}
{/* Call to Action Widget */}
